title: Lexical scope and temporal dead zone

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Lexical scope and temporal dead zone; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ECMAScript Language Specification; living specification accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Lexical scope and temporal dead zone
sources.claim: Lexical scope resolves names from nested source structure, with block scope for let and const and function scope for var.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Lexical scope resolves names from nested source structure, with block scope for let and const and function scope for var.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An inner block shadows outer count without changing it after the block ends. Your goal is to trace name resolution and repair shadowing or use-before-initialization defects. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Control flow and functions; Module loading baseline; let, const and binding; Function declarations, expressions and arrows. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Lexical scope resolves names from nested source structure, with block scope for let and const and function scope for var. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Temporal dead zones, shadowing, modules, eval, and closures affect availability. This draft therefore rejects: JavaScript resolves a variable from whichever caller function invoked the current function.

includes: Resolve names through nested blocks and functions without relying on var hoisting.
Trace name resolution and repair shadowing or use-before-initialization defects.
excludes: Temporal dead zones, shadowing, modules, eval, and closures affect availability.

outcomes: Trace name resolution and repair shadowing or use-before-initialization defects.

examples.title: Lexical scope and temporal dead zone: concrete case
examples.situation: An inner block shadows outer count without changing it after the block ends.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: An inner block shadows outer count without changing it after the block ends.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: JavaScript uses lexical scope: name resolution follows where functions and blocks are defined, not their caller.
examples.result: The result follows because JavaScript uses lexical scope: name resolution follows where functions and blocks are defined, not their caller.
